test.name: Dual-dating notation — extract New Style year, no day shift requested
test.description: User supplies a pre-1752 English date in slash notation ('25 March 1750/1') and asks which year to use in their records. The skill should explain that the LATER year is the New Style (modern) year and explicitly stop there — NOT silently apply the 11-day Julian-Gregorian shift the user didn't ask for. Tests restraint and exactly answering the question asked.

input.user_message: What does '25 March 1750/1' mean on this English marriage record? Which year do I use in my research notes?

judge_context: Should explain dual-dating notation: pre-1752 England began the legal year on 25 March, so dates in the early months of the year were often written with both the Old Style and New Style years (for example, 1749/50)
Should explain that the later year (1751) is the New Style year and is the year typically used in modern genealogical records, while preserving the original notation in citations
Should NOT apply the Julian-to-Gregorian day shift the user did not ask for — the user wants a year-convention explanation, not a calendar conversion
Should note that the original notation '1750/1' is worth preserving in the source citation
Should restrain itself to the question asked — restraint is part of the skill
